title: One Five-Minute Daily Anchor
subtitle: A minimal daily spiritual practice for the woman who doesn't know where to start
excerpt: A simple ritual for building a daily spiritual practice when you are not sure what that means for you yet. One five-minute anchor, done consistently, is enough to create the structure that more complex practice can grow from.
sourceNotes: Written for homemade.education. The principle of one consistent daily anchor as the basis of a spiritual practice appears across many traditions from Buddhist morning practice to Christian Daily Office to secular contemplative traditions.

practice.whenToUse: Use as a daily morning practice, especially when you want to build a spiritual life but are not sure where to start.

content.text: A minimal daily practice for the woman who knows she wants some kind of spiritual anchor but doesn't know what tradition, what form, or what it should look like. The practice is simple: one thing, every morning, for five minutes. That's it.

Choose your anchor. It can be one thing from this list or something else entirely: sit quietly for five minutes, say one sentence of gratitude, read one paragraph from a book that feeds you, write three sentences in a journal, say a prayer in whatever form fits, light a candle and watch it. The content matters less than the consistency. Same time. Same place. Every day.
I am ready to release the idea that my spiritual practice needs to be more than this. I release it now. I release it now. I release it now.
I am ready to allow five minutes a day to be a real and sufficient spiritual practice. I allow it now. I allow it now. I allow it now.
Do the anchor. All five minutes of it. Don't check your phone. Don't add anything else to it. When the five minutes are done, the practice is done.
This is enough. Done consistently, this is enough.
Then go and start the day. Come back tomorrow.

Written for homemade.education. The principle of one consistent daily anchor appears across many spiritual traditions: the five daily prayers of Islam, the Daily Office in Christian traditions, the morning sitting practice in Zen Buddhism, secular MBSR morning meditation. In each case the consistency of one practice, done daily, is the foundation of a spiritual life.
